grounding_source_rows: human_disagreements rows 145, 152, 156; taxonomy Consumer Law descriptions
grounding_note: Consumer Law's specific labels (Storage Units, Vehicle Towing, Banking, Identity Theft) versus the General fallback split on facts about who did what: which business, which practice, which statute. Rows 145/152/156 show humans defaulting to General when the specific hook wasn't visible. The specific hook is always a fact the user can state.

scenarios.opening_query: All my belongings are about to be sold by a company that has no right to do this.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > General || Consumer Law > Storage Units
scenarios.hidden_fact: It's my storage unit — they claim I'm behind on fees and posted a lien-sale notice, but I have receipts showing I paid.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's my storage unit — they posted a lien-sale notice claiming missed fees, but I have receipts showing I paid.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a storage-unit lien sale is the mechanism
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's a furniture store 'repossessing' a paid-off couch over a billing error — no storage anything.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's a furniture store trying to take back a paid-off couch over their billing error.
scenarios.vetting_note: Storage lien sales have their own statute and label; 'about to be sold' hides the mechanism.

scenarios.opening_query: My car disappeared from where I parked it and getting it back has cost me a fortune and my dignity.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > General || Consumer Law > Vehicle Towing
scenarios.hidden_fact: A patrol tow company took it from my own apartment lot despite my valid permit — then charged $780 and 'lost' my tools from the trunk.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: A patrol tow took it from my own lot despite a valid permit — $780 to get it back and my tools are 'missing.'
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether an improper tow and impound practices are the issue
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It wasn't towed — the dealership 'recovered' it claiming my financing fell through after I drove off with signed papers.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: Not towed — the dealer took it back claiming my financing fell through after I'd signed everything.
scenarios.vetting_note: Tow-specific practices vs yo-yo financing; both fit 'car disappeared.'

scenarios.opening_query: My bank has been taking money out of my account in ways I never agreed to.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Banking || Consumer Law > Identity Theft
scenarios.hidden_fact: The withdrawals trace to a loan someone opened in my name with a fake ID — the bank says the loan is 'mine' and keeps debiting payments.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: They trace to a loan someone opened in my name with fake ID — the bank insists it's mine and keeps debiting.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether fraudulent identity use created the obligation
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's the bank's own doing — stacked 'maintenance' and overdraft fees on an account they promised was free.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's the bank itself — stacked fees on an account they sold as free.
scenarios.vetting_note: Fraud-origin vs bank-practice; the loan's provenance is the routing fact.

scenarios.opening_query: We paid a company thousands to get us out of our timeshare and they did absolutely nothing.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > General || Real Property > Other
scenarios.hidden_fact: Beyond the exit-company scam, the timeshare itself keeps assessing us — we're deeded owners of a fractional real-estate interest and need the ownership itself unwound.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's worse than the scam — we're deeded owners of the fractional interest and it keeps assessing us. The ownership itself has to be unwound.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the deeded timeshare interest itself needs unwinding beyond the scam refund
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The resort actually released us last year — the only fight left is getting our money back from the exit company.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The resort released us last year — the only fight is the exit company's fee.
scenarios.vetting_note: Row 145 verbatim (timeshare + exit-company); humans split Consumer General vs Real Property Other on exactly this.

scenarios.opening_query: A company charged my card for a year of service I cancelled and refuses every refund request.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > General || Consumer Law > Banking
scenarios.hidden_fact: The deeper problem is my own bank — I filed proper dispute paperwork within the deadline and they rejected the chargeback without ever investigating, which I'm told violates their obligations.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The real problem is my bank — I filed the dispute on time and they rejected the chargeback with zero investigation, which apparently violates their obligations.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the bank's dispute-handling failure is the operative claim
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The bank did its part and refunded — the fight is with the merchant, who then sent the 'debt' to collections.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The bank refunded fine — the merchant's the problem, and they sent it to collections.
scenarios.vetting_note: Merchant-vs-bank defendant selection; users experience it as one problem.

scenarios.opening_query: Someone filed a tax return using my information and now my whole financial life is frozen.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Identity Theft || Taxation > Personal Income
scenarios.hidden_fact: The identity part is contained — IRS flagged it and issued me a PIN. What's stuck is my own legitimate refund and a proposed assessment from the mess, and I need the tax side resolved.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The identity part's contained — I have an IRS PIN now. What's stuck is my real refund and a proposed assessment from the mess.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the remaining fight is with the IRS over the account rather than the fraud
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's far beyond taxes — they opened three cards and a car loan in my name, and collectors are circling; I need the full identity-theft response.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's way beyond taxes — three cards and a car loan in my name, collectors circling.
scenarios.vetting_note: Scope disclosure: contained-fraud-remaining-tax-mess vs active multi-account theft.

scenarios.opening_query: The moving and storage company is holding our household goods hostage for charges we never agreed to.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Storage Units || Administrative Law > Transportation || Consumer Law > General
scenarios.hidden_fact: It was an interstate move — the mover is a federally regulated carrier, and the weight-based charges violate the tariff rules their federal registration requires.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It was an interstate move — they're a federally regulated carrier and these weight charges violate their own tariff rules.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the mover is a regulated interstate carrier
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's the local storage facility where our things ended up — they're claiming lien rights over disputed fees and threatening a sale.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's the local storage facility — claiming lien rights over disputed fees and threatening to sell our things.
scenarios.vetting_note: Interstate-carrier status invokes transportation regulation; 'moving and storage' straddles both.

scenarios.opening_query: An online seller took my money for equipment that never arrived and now the website is gone.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > General || Consumer Law > Problems Between Consumers
scenarios.hidden_fact: It wasn't a store — it was an individual on a marketplace app selling his used camera gear; $2,300 by payment app, person to person.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It was an individual on a marketplace app — his used camera gear, $2,300 person-to-person by payment app.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the seller was a business or a private individual
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It was a registered online retailer with a warehouse and everything — they just folded overnight.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It was a real registered retailer — they just folded overnight.
scenarios.vetting_note: Business-vs-individual seller again but in the online context where it's genuinely invisible.
